journal-title-group.journal-title: European Heart Journal Supplements: Journal of the European Society of Cardiology

title-group.article-title: Optimizing protected PCI strategies in the setting of chronic kidney disease and advanced coronary artery disease: a clinical case-based discussion

article-meta.issue-title: Protected PCI – Contemporary Evidence & Experience in Perspective

All other permissions can be obtained through our RightsLink service via the Permissions link on the article page on our site—for further information please contact journals.permissions@oup.com.

abstract.title: Abstract
abstract.p: Patients with chronic coronary artery disease (CAD) and severely reduced left ventricular ejection fraction (LVEF) represent a particularly high-risk population often deemed unsuitable for conventional revascularization strategies. Procedural challenges arise from complex coronary anatomy, impaired cardiac reserve, and frequent comorbidities, such as frailty and chronic kidney disease, all of which predispose patients to haemodynamic instability and adverse outcomes during percutaneous coronary intervention (PCI). The use of mechanical circulatory support (MCS) devices, such as a microaxial flow pump (mAFP), has emerged as an important strategy to mitigate these risks by providing continuous left ventricular unloading and augmenting cardiac output. Moreover, improved perfusion may attenuate the risk of acute kidney injury (AKI), a major determinant of post-PCI morbidity and mortality. Herein, we present a case of Impella™-supported PCI in an elderly male patient with multivessel CAD, markedly reduced LVEF, chronic kidney disease, and multiple comorbidities, highlighting the role of MCS in optimizing procedural safety and expanding revascularization options for this vulnerable patient population.

caption.p: Optimizing protected PCI strategies in patients with advanced CAD. CAD, coronary artery disease; PCI, percutaneous coronary intervention; Post-Op, post-operative.

graphic.alt-text: For image description, please refer to the figure legend and surrounding text.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Patients with chronic coronary artery disease (CAD) and significantly reduced left ventricular ejection fraction (LVEF) are under-treated with both percutaneous coronary intervention (PCI) and coronary artery bypass grafting (CABG) due to the elevated risk of procedural complications and adverse outcomes. This high-risk cohort often presents with complex coronary anatomy and concomitant comorbidities, such as frailty and chronic kidney disease, that further exacerbate vulnerability during surgical or percutaneous interventions. The severe left ventricular systolic dysfunction impairs cardiac reserve, predisposing patients to haemodynamic instability and acute decompensation during PCI, thereby limiting the ability to achieve optimal and complete revascularization.1

Implementation of mechanical circulatory support (MCS) devices has emerged as a pivotal strategy in this high-risk setting. The microaxial flow pump (mAFP) provides percutaneous left ventricular unloading and active circulatory support by directly augmenting cardiac output. By stabilizing haemodynamics in real time, the mAFP allows operators to perform complex and prolonged interventions with reduced risk of ischaemic and haemodynamic complications. This is particularly important in patients with severely reduced LVEF, in whom transient episodes of hypotension can precipitate irreversible myocardial injury and multiorgan dysfunction.1

Furthermore, the risk of acute kidney injury (AKI) in this cohort is multifactorial, often arising from pre-existing renal impairment, contrast exposure, and haemodynamic instability. By improving cardiac output and maintaining renal perfusion, the Impella CP™ may help attenuate the incidence and severity of AKI, which is a known independent predictor of morbidity and mortality post-PCI. In addition, MCS may address the challenges posed by frailty, a common yet under-recognized factor influencing procedural tolerance and recovery, by providing cardiovascular support that compensates for diminished physiological reserve. Herein, we describe a high-risk patient with severe multivessel CAD and multiple comorbidities who was treated successfully with Impella-supported PCI.1

sec.title: Patient presentation and background
sec.p: A 72-year-old male with a complex medical background, including stage 4 chronic kidney disease with an estimated glomerular filtration rate (eGFR) of 25 mL/min/1.73 m², severe chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD), New York Heart Association (NYHA) class III symptoms, and significant immobility due to an iatrogenic right sciatic nerve injury sustained during prior orthopaedic surgery, with a Clinical Frailty Scale (CFS) score of 4, presented with symptoms consistent with acute coronary syndrome.

sec.title: Baseline cardiovascular evaluation and risk assessment
sec.p: At admission, he was hypotensive and bradycardic, and electrocardiography revealed complete atrioventricular (AV) block and signs of anterior ischaemia. Echocardiography also demonstrated a globally hypokinetic LVEF of 20%, characterized by diffuse hypokinesia and anterio-apical wall akinesia, moderate mitral regurgitation, and preserved right ventricle function. Coronary angiography (Figure 1) revealed critical three-vessel disease involving the left main stem, a chronic total occlusion (CTO) of the proximal left anterior descending (LAD) artery, severe calcific stenosis in the proximal-to-mid left circumflex (LCx) artery, and significant disease in the right coronary artery (RCA). The LAD was occluded from its origin with poor collateral filling from the RCA, and the left main showed diffuse disease extending into the bifurcation (SYNTAX score of 41). Lower limb angiography showed no significant atherosclerosis, with preserved femoral artery calibres. Cardiac magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) demonstrated subepicardial viability in the anterior wall [late gadolinium enhancement (LGE) <50% in the anterior/apical wall], supporting an attempt at complete revascularization. Given the prohibitive surgical risk due to pulmonary disease, renal impairment, and frailty, the Heart Team elected to pursue a percutaneous revascularization strategy with MCS.

caption.p: Baseline coronary angiography. (A) Right coronary artery; (B) LAO 45°/CAU 25° left coronary artery; (C) RAO 30°/CRAN 30° left coronary artery; and (D) RAO 25°/CAU 25° left coronary artery. CAU, caudal; CRAN, cranial; LAO, left anterior oblique; RAO, right anterior oblique.

graphic.alt-text: For image description, please refer to the figure legend and surrounding text.

sec.title: Procedural strategy
sec.p: For this case, a staged approach was adopted. First, a cardiac resynchronization therapy defibrillator (CRT-D) was implanted to address the AV block and optimize cardiac resynchronization in the setting of reduced LVEF. Subsequently, protected PCI was proposed to achieve complete and optimal revascularization with intravascular ultrasound (IVUS) guidance, plaque debulking of the LCx, and attempted CTO PCI. Specific hydration management with RenalGuard was performed to reduce the risk of AKI, and the patient underwent a single-acces protected PCI using an Impella CP device inserted via echocardiography-guided femoral access.

sec.title: Interventional procedure summary
sec.p: Initial revascularization targeted the RCA to augment collateral perfusion for the LAD. After conventional balloon pre-dilatation, three drug-eluting stents (DES; 2.5 × 38 mm, 3.0 × 38 mm, and 3.5 × 12 mm) were implanted via a 6 Fr child-in-mother system, achieving satisfactory angiographic and IVUS results (Figure 2A). Treatment of the LCx was subsequently performed using a 1.75 mm rotational atherectomy burr for lesion preparation, prompted by the lack of dilatation due to a calcific plaque in the distal segment (Figure 2B). This was followed by two IVUS-guided DES implantations (3.5 × 38 mm and 4.0 × 38 mm) in the mid-to-distal segments and one DES (3.0 mm × 38 mm) in the first obtuse marginal branch using a T and small-protrusion technique (Figure 2C).

caption.p: Protected PCI procedure. (A) Result after right coronary artery PCI; (B) Rotational atherectomy on left circumflex; (C) Result after left circumflex PCI; (D) Rotation atherectomy on left anterior descending; (E) RAO 25°/CAU 25° result after PCI; and (F) RAO 30°/CRA° result after PCI. CAU, caudal; CRA, cranial; PCI, percutaneous coronary intervention; RAO, right anterior oblique.

graphic.alt-text: For image description, please refer to the figure legend and surrounding text.

sec.p: Recanalization of the LAD was performed antegradly using a stepwise approach. Initial wiring attempts were made with a Fielder XT-A and Gaia Second wire supported by a Finecross 130 cm microcatheter. Pre-dilatation was performed using sequential non-compliant (NC) balloons. Due to the presence of an extensive, uncrossable calcific burden in the mid-LAD segments, a 1.75 mm burr was used to perform rotational atherectomy, followed by high-pressure balloon inflation with 3.0 mm NC balloons (Figure 2D).

Next, IVUS imaging was employed to optimize lesion assessment, revealing a long, diffusely calcified segment, eccentric plaque, and under-expansion after the initial ballooning. Two overlapping DES (2.5 mm × 34.0 mm and 2.75 mm × 30.0 mm) were deployed from the proximal LAD through the mid-segment, with post-dilatation performed using a 3.0 and 4.0 mm NC balloon under high pressure. The left main bifurcation was subsequently treated using a culotte stenting technique, indicated by diffuse disease extending to the proximal LAD and LCx. Two DES (3.5 × 38.0 and 4.0 mm × 38.0 mm) were implanted, followed by post-dilatation with NC 4.0 mm and 4.5 mm balloons and final proximal optimization technique with a 5.5 mm NC balloon. A final IVUS confirmed optimal stent expansion and apposition in both branches, with adequate expansion in the left main (stent area >12 mm²) and restoration of TIMI 3 flow (Figure 2E and F).

Impella CP support was maintained at pump power (P) level P6 for the duration of the procedure to prevent left ventricular adaptation and was increased to P9 during rotational atherectomy and left main PCI to counteract transient loss of pulsatility. Clinical stability was maintained throughout the procedure, without an increase in lactate level, and the total contrast volume remained relatively low at 200 cc.

sec.title: Post-procedural course and outcomes
sec.p: Following the procedure, the patient was closely monitored in a coronary intensive care unit. Haemodynamic stability was maintained, with only a modest rise in serum creatinine post-intervention (2.2 mg/dL to a peak of 2.7 mg/dL) that did not warrant renal replacement therapy. No peri-procedural myocardial infarction or major bleeding occurred. Subsequent Impella weaning was rapid, with no significant changes in mean arterial pressure when decreasing support from P6 to P3. The procedure only required mild sedation, and the post-procedural transthoracic echocardiogram confirmed the absence of any complications. The Impella was removed 24 h post-PCI and the access site was closed with a single pre-implanted ProGlide™.

At discharge, the patient demonstrated clinical improvement in exertional capacity and resolution of anginal symptoms. A tailored rehabilitation programme was instituted per institutional protocol. At the 6-month follow-up visit, the patient reported no cardiovascular events. His NYHA class improved to II, LVEF increased to 33%, mitral regurgitation showed mild improvement, and right ventricle function was normal.

sec.title: Evidence-based discussion and implications for practice
Patient presentation and background
sec.p: Patient presentation plays a pivotal role in protected PCI, as clinical status, haemodynamic stability, and comorbidity burden determine procedural strategy and device selection to achieve the best possible outcome. One important consideration is age, as elderly patients (≥65 years of age) with ventricular dysfunction, valvular disease, and comorbidities such as chronic kidney disease, COPD, and frailty have markedly reduced physiological reserves. Moreover, frailty is a strong independent predictor of mortality after both surgery and PCI, with risk increasing in proportion to frailty severity, even after adjustment for age and comorbidities.2,3 The CFS provides simple and reliable stratification, outperforming conventional surgical scores and remaining valid in patients undergoing revascularization with MCS.2–5 Importantly, frailty not only predicts early procedural risk but also limits the long-term benefit of invasive strategies, as functional decline, reduced mobility, and vulnerability to complications often outweigh potential survival gains.3 In this context, careful integration of frailty assessment into Heart Team discussions can help to identify patients in whom complex PCI or surgery may be futile and redirect therapeutic decisions toward symptom relief and preservation of quality of life.

In patients with advanced age and major comorbidities, the net survival benefit of invasive strategies may diminish, as uncaptured vulnerabilities such as frailty contribute to excess mortality. In these settings, individualized judgment by the Heart Team that integrates frailty, age, comorbidities, anatomy, and haemodynamics provides a more accurate prognosis than conventional scores.6 As such, guideline-driven endpoints may not align with patient priorities,7 underscoring the importance of shared decision-making. For many elderly patients, mAFP–assisted PCI enables complete revascularization comparable to surgery, with lower procedural burden and more favourable survival, while remaining consistent with patient expectations.8–10

Renal dysfunction is another critical determinant of outcome. In the current case, one advantage of using Impella was related to its favourable effect on renal perfusion in patients undergoing high-risk PCI. However, in patients with cardiogenic shock undergoing high-risk PCI, Impella use may be complicated by AKI.11–14 This discrepancy may be explained by shorter support times, reduced suction events through the AUTO mode in traditional high-risk PCI cases (the default setting during the first three hours of operation), and the ability of Impella to preserve renal perfusion during haemodynamic instability. Equally important is renal congestion, a condition that is now recognized as a major pathophysiological driver of worsening renal function in heart failure and appears to be alleviated by mAFP support through reductions in left ventricular filling pressures and central venous congestion, thereby improving kidney haemodynamics.15–17 This mechanism, by which Impella unloading decreases renal sympathetic nerve activity and enhances perfusion, has also been shown in preclinical animal models.18 Importantly, despite the renal benefits of an mAFP, the most effective strategy for preventing AKI remains peri-procedural intravascular hydration, which can be guided effectively by left ventricular end-diastolic pressure, urine flow rate, or right heart catheterization.19–21

sec.title: Baseline cardiovascular evaluation and risk assessment
sec.p: In this clinical case, the combination of severely depressed LVEF and CAD, including a left main lesion and LAD CTO leading to a high SYNTAX score,22 describes a high-risk multimorbid patient. Although there is no universally accepted definition of high-risk PCI, the combination of complex CAD, reduced ventricular function, and comorbidity burden characterizes patients at increased risk for haemodynamic instability and hypoperfusion during PCI, which prevents optimal and complete revascularization.23 Therefore, assessment of left ventricular function and relevant valvular disease by echocardiography prior to PCI is important. Both reduced LVEF and significant mitral regurgitation are predictors of decompensation and haemodynamic collapse during high-risk PCI.24 Although technically not impossible, severe aortic stenosis is still a contraindication for Impella placement according to the device instructions for use. Moreover, echocardiography allows assessment of right ventricular function and tricuspid regurgitation, which is essential since Impella is a left ventricular assist device and relies on a preserved right ventricular function. In a randomized clinical trial (RCT), the PROTECT IV trial, severe tricuspid regurgitation and clinically overt right heart failure are exclusion criteria.25

In the setting of ischaemic cardiomyopathy, MRI is typically performed for assessment of viability and/or inducible ischaemia. In our clinical case, viability in the LAD territory was confirmed by MRI. However, to date, no RCT has established the clinical benefit of ischaemia and viability testing to guide revascularization in patients with heart failure with reduced ejection fraction (HFrEF). As such, analyses of the STICH viability sub-study showed that the presence or absence of myocardial viability did not identify patients who derived benefit from CABG over medical therapy alone.26,27 Viability was also not independently associated with death at 5 or 10 years.26,27 Although viable myocardium was linked to improvements in LVEF with both CABG plus optimal medical therapy and optimal medical therapy alone, these changes did not translate into better clinical outcomes.26 In the REVIVED-BCIS2 trial, patients were required to undergo viability testing via MRI or stress echocardiography.28 Across the entire trial population, the median extent of viable and non-viable myocardium was 29% [interquartile range (IQR) 12–53%] and 29% (IQR, 12–41%), respectively. Viability testing did not identify which patients would benefit from PCI, as there was no interaction between myocardial viability and the effect of PCI on the primary or secondary outcomes. Event-free survival and the likelihood of improvement in left ventricular function were associated with the extent of non-viable myocardium, but not with the amount of viable myocardium.29

Similar to myocardial viability, the presence or absence of inducible ischaemia did not identify patients with worse prognosis or those who would derive a greater beneﬁt from revascularization, either with CABG in a STICH trial sub-study30 or with PCI in REVIVED-BCIS2.31 The main findings of the International Study of Comparative Health Effectiveness With Medical and Invasive Approaches (ISCHAEMIA) study do not apply to patients with severely reduced LVEF, as these patients were excluded from the trial.32 However, a sub-study of the ISCHAEMIA trial found that patients with a history of symptomatic heart failure and LVEF of 35% to 45% who received invasive therapy had better outcomes than those who received conservative therapy.33 While these data are promising, the results must be considered hypothesis-generating only due to the non-randomized design and small sample size. Moreover, these findings call into question the usefulness of myocardial viability and ischaemia as prerequisite inclusion criteria for RCTs, and their clinical relevance. Despite these data, however, the most recent European Association of Percutaneous Cardiovascular Interventions (EAPCI) most recent consensus on high-risk PCI34 and CTO35 still considers myocardial viability a reliable clinical indicator in this patient population.

Lastly, acute and chronic coronary syndromes often overlap in patients undergoing protected PCI, as illustrated by this patient, who presented with non-ST-elevation myocardial infarction (NSTEMI). In the PROTECT III trial, patients often present with a combination of CAD—characterized by multivessel involvement, high SYNTAX scores, and reduced LVEF—and acute features, such as recent myocardial infarction or unstable angina. This overlap increases the risk of haemodynamic compromise during PCI, especially in those with severely depressed LVEF (median 27–30%) and extensive ischaemic burden.36 Clinically, these patients frequently have comorbidities, such as diabetes, renal dysfunction, and/or prior heart failure, which further elevate procedural risk. Reduced LVEF and significant mitral regurgitation are key predictors of peri-procedural haemodynamic instability, as both impair cardiac output and increase susceptibility to decompensation during complex interventions.36 It is important to note that in the PROTECT III trial, indications for Impella device placement were largely driven by a combination of clinical, haemodynamic, and anatomical risk factors, particularly in the context of overlapping acute and chronic coronary syndromes, while the presence of just one of these characteristics alone was uncommon in this patient population.37

sec.title: Procedural strategy
sec.p: Adequate procedural planning for protected PCI is essential for successful intervention. Vascular complications remain a significant drawback in patients undergoing protected high-risk PCI. The use of large-bore access for temporary MCS (tMCS) is likely associated with increased bleeding and access-site complications. In the setting of a (semi-)elective procedure, pre-procedural imaging of vascular access via computed tomography or ultrasound is highly recommended. Moreover, in the randomized PROTECT IV trial, patient inclusion is dependent on pre-procedural imaging of the vascular access site.25 This is because, if the first vessel is not suitable, adding a second vascular access site increases the risk of bleeding complications.38 Furthermore, a recent meta-analysis highlighted a more than 50% reduction in vascular complications with ultrasound-guided vascular access compared with a non-guided approach.39

Unlike upfront imaging, the routine use of right heart catheterization when performing high-risk protected PCI remains to be explored. Characterization of the haemodynamic profile in patients with cardiogenic shock using a pulmonary artery catheter has been associated with reduced in-hospital mortality.40 Whether a similar outcome can be extended to high-risk PCI is yet to be determined. In our case, we elected to monitor the patient’s haemodynamics using blood pressure and lactate measurements. Impella support was tailored to procedural steps, enabling the operator to achieve complete revascularization in a patient with multivessel disease and poor left ventricular function. Moreover, the improved haemodynamic stability provided by the presence of mAFP support enabled the use of calcium modification therapies, such as rotational atherectomy.8 A more successful PCI procedure, defined as complete revascularization with adequate lesion preparation and optimal stent expansion, is more likely to occur with the use of an mAFP. In fact, this is the prevailing hypothesis as to why mAFP is associated with fewer cardiovascular events over time.41

sec.title: Interventional procedure summary
sec.p: In our case, the intra-procedural course was clinically uneventful. However, during rotational atherectomy, loss of pulsatility was identified on the Impella console. Approximately half of high-risk PCI patients experience loss of pulse pressure during the procedure, and these patients have a worse prognosis than those without loss of pulsatility.42

Typically, most patients undergoing Impella-supported high-risk PCI are weaned at the end of the procedure, and the Impella device is removed in the catheterization laboratory. Data from a German registry demonstrated that Impella was explanted in the catheterization laboratory in 87% of cases.43 Currently, there are no scientifically established criteria outlining the timing and execution of weaning, removal of support, or when to keep the patient on support after high-risk PCI. While standard practice suggests monitoring arterial blood pressure, blood chemistry (e.g. lactate), echocardiography, and advanced haemodynamics from right heart catheterization (RHC),44 there are no clear cut-off values available to guide decisions on weaning and safe removal of support. In the randomized PROTECT IV trial, an RHC sub-study will collect numerous RHC haemodynamic measurements at various timepoints throughout high-risk PCI, including before, during, and after PCI, and is anticipated to provide important data to guide Impella weaning.25 Despite the lack of consensus on decision-making for intra-procedural Impella-supported high-risk PCI, intra-procedural observation is critical to ensure that any bleeding, vascular, or structural complications that occur during the procedure receive immediate interventional treatment to achieve the best outcome.45

sec.title: Impella management and weaning mandatory minute ventilation
sec.p: The present case illustrates the feasibility and safety of short-term Impella CP support to facilitate high-risk multivessel PCI in a frail patient with severe left ventricular dysfunction. By maintaining circulatory stability throughout the procedure, the Impella device allowed completion of a complex revascularization strategy that included rotational atherectomy and left main PCI.

A key principle emphasized here is the titration of P-levels according to procedural demands. Support was maintained at P6 during most of the case to avoid left ventricular pressure adaptation, with brief escalation to P9 during the most critical phases. Importantly, haemodynamic monitoring, such as mean arterial pressure (MAP), lactate trends, and echocardiographic parameters, guided intra-procedural adjustments and allowed safe downtitration without circulatory compromise. This supports prior evidence that optimal device utilization requires dynamic adaptation rather than fixed-level support.46,47

Moreover, the weaning strategy adopted highlights the value of SmartAssist™ parameters in combination with clinical and echocardiographic assessment. Criteria such as stable MAP without vasoactive support, absence of suction events, stable motor current, and reassuring lactate and urine output trends were used to trigger P-level reduction. A structured pause at lower support levels (P2–P3) ensured haemodynamic autonomy before explantation. These measures align with proposed algorithms advocating gradual unloading withdrawal, integrating invasive pressure monitoring with device-derived metrics.48

Bedside echocardiography provided an additional safeguard both immediately post-PCI and pre-explantation. Beyond left ventricular function, systematic right ventricular evaluation was critical to exclude evolving right ventricular dysfunction, which could complicate Impella withdrawal because of increased pulmonary pressures and interventricular interdependence. Attention to valve competence, device orientation, and pericardial fluid further strengthened the safety net. Thus, this multi-modal approach, combining invasive, device-derived, and imaging signals, mirrors best-practice recommendations for MCS management.49

Finally, vascular access and closure strategy deserve emphasis. Planning for explantation at the time of implantation is a crucial component of safe large-bore access management. In this case, the use of a single pre-close ProGlide device enabled uncomplicated haemostasis after device removal, with structured post-procedural surveillance minimizing vascular complications. This approach reinforces that vascular strategy is foundational, not secondary, to the safety, effectiveness, and ultimate success of an Impella-supported PCI programme.

sec.title: Post-procedural course and outcomes
sec.p: In the present case, haemodynamic stability was maintained throughout the procedure, no in-hospital adverse events occurred, and, at 6-month follow-up, the patient demonstrated improved cardiovascular status without recurrent events, reflecting both the safety and effectiveness of Impella-supported PCI. Similarly, in the PROTECT III trial, the 90-day rate of major adverse cardiac and cerebrovascular events (MACCE) was 15.1%, significantly lower than the 21.9% observed in PROTECT II (P = 0.037). After propensity score matching, the 90-day MACCE rate further improved to 10.4% in PROTECT III vs. 16.9% in PROTECT II (P = 0.048).36 Meta-analyses and registry data corroborate these findings, showing acceptable 6-month mortality rates (13–18%), and low rates of myocardial infarction (5–6%), stroke (1–3%), and heart failure rehospitalization (8%) in high-risk populations with overlapping acute and chronic syndromes.50,51 Importantly, Impella support enabled more aggressive revascularization strategies in anatomically complex patients, with improvements in left ventricular function and ischaemic burden.52 Overall, Impella-supported PCI in PROTECT III was associated with reduced peri-procedural complications and improved short-term outcomes in high-risk patients with overlapping acute and chronic coronary syndromes.52 Collectively, these findings highlight the advances of contemporary Impella-supported PCI in achieving complete revascularization and improving procedural safety.

Guideline-directed medical therapy after protected high-risk PCI in patients with CAD and low ejection fraction should include dual antiplatelet therapy. More specifically, this should include aspirin plus a P2Y12 inhibitor such as clopidogrel, prasugrel, or ticagrelor for at least 12 months, followed by indefinite aspirin monotherapy, unless bleeding risk is high.53 Optimal medical therapy for heart failure should be continued, including beta-blockers, angiotensin-converting enzyme (ACE) inhibitors, angiotensin receptor blockers (ARBs), angiotensin receptor-neprilysin inhibitor (ARNI), mineralocorticoid receptor antagonists, and sodium-glucose cotransporter 2 (SGLT2) inhibitors as tolerated.54 Beta-blockers (e.g. carvedilol, metoprolol succinate, bisoprolol) should be titrated to target doses as tolerated. ACE inhibitors or ARBs (or sacubitril/valsartan for eligible patients) are recommended for all patients with reduced ejection fraction, unless contraindicated. Mineralocorticoid receptor antagonists (e.g. spironolactone, eplerenone) and SGLT2 inhibitors (e.g. dapagliflozin, empagliflozin) further reduce morbidity and mortality. High-intensity statin therapy is recommended for all patients, with consideration of ezetimibe or proprotein convertase subtilisin/kexin type 9 (PCSK9) inhibitors if low-density lipoprotein targets are not met.55 Device therapy, such as an implantable cardioverter-defibrillator or CRT-D, should be considered in eligible patients with persistently low ejection fraction despite optimal medical therapy.28

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: This clinical case demonstrates how a Heart Team-guided, Impella-supported PCI strategy can provide a viable alternative to surgery in elderly, frail patients with multivessel disease and comorbidities (Graphical Abstract). By combining haemodynamic support, intravascular imaging, and strategic procedural planning, the Heart Team achieved complete revascularization with low contrast use and preserved renal function despite advanced chronic kidney disease. This case and the supporting literature reinforce the importance of pre-procedural planning, multidisciplinary coordination, meticulous vascular and renal management, and individualized intra-procedural strategy development in optimizing outcomes for complex coronary disease.

ack.title: Acknowledgements
ack.p: This manuscript is one of eight manuscripts published as a Supplement to address “Protected PCI – Contemporary Evidence and Experience in Perspective”. JetPub Scientific Communications, LLC, supported by funding from Johnson & Johnson MedTech HeartRecovery, provided editorial assistance to the authors during preparation of this supplement.

sec.title: Funding
sec.p: This paper was published as part of a supplement financially supported by Johnson & Johnson MedTech HeartRecovery.

sec.title: Data availability
sec.p: The data underlying this article will be shared upon reasonable request to the corresponding author.
